Sample: RANSOM_002 | Category: Ransomware
Evidence:
- [E1] (other) [E1] Encrypts files with AES-256 and appends a custom extension
- [E2] (other) [E2] Deletes original files after encryption
- [E3] (other) [E3] Drops a ransom note named "READMEtxt" in each encrypted directory
- [E4] (other) [E4] Modifies the registry to run on startup
- [E5] (other) [E5] Attempts to spread to network shares

Sample: RANSOM_003 | Category: Ransomware
Evidence:
- [E1] (other) [E1] Encrypts files with AES-256 and appends a custom extension
- [E2] (other) [E2] Drops a ransom note with payment instructions and a unique ID
- [E3] (other) [E3] Communicates with a C2 server to retrieve encryption keys
- [E4] (other) [E4] Deletes shadow copies and attempts to disable antivirus software

Sample: INFO_002 | Category: Infostealer
Evidence:
- [E1] (other) [E1] Reads browser profile databases and extracts saved login records
- [E2] (other) [E2] Accesses OS credential store / keychain APIs
- [E3] (other) [E3] Compresses stolen data into a single archive for staging
- [E4] (other) [E4] Makes outbound POST requests with the archive to a remote endpoint
- [E5] (other) [E5] Steals cryptocurrency wallet files

Sample: INFO_003 | Category: Infostealer
Evidence:
- [E1] (other) [E1] Collects system information, including OS version, hostname, and user domain
- [E2] (other) [E2] Enumerates running processes and their associated modules
- [E3] (other) [E3] Searches for and exfiltrates files with specific extensions (eg, doc, xls, pdf)
- [E4] (other) [E4] Establishes persistence by creating a scheduled task
- [E5] (other) [E5] Communicates with a C2 server to receive commands and send stolen data

Sample: BD_002 | Category: Backdoor
Evidence:
- [E1] (other) [E1] Establishes a persistent connection to a C2 server
- [E2] (other) [E2] Allows remote execution of arbitrary code
- [E3] (other) [E3] Downloads and executes additional payloads
- [E4] (other) [E4] Hides its presence by masquerading as a legitimate system process

Sample: BD_003 | Category: Backdoor
Evidence:
- [E1] (other) [E1] Creates a scheduled task to maintain persistence
- [E2] (other) [E2] Downloads and executes additional payloads
- [E3] (other) [E3] Communicates with a C2 server over HTTP

Sample: MALWARE_001 | Category: Malware
Evidence:
- [E1] (other) [E1] Attempts to disable security software by modifying registry keys
- [E2] (other) [E2] Connects to a suspicious IP address over HTTP for command and control
- [E3] (other) [E3] Downloads and executes an unsigned executable file from a remote server
- [E4] (other) [E4] Injects code into legitimate system processes to evade detection

Sample: MALWARE_001 | Category: Malware
Evidence:
- [E1] (network) [E1] Connects to a known command and control (C2) server at 1921681100
- [E2] (file) [E2] Creates a new file named "keylogtxt" in the user's Documents folder
- [E3] (process) [E3] Spawns a new process with the name "svchostexe" that exhibits suspicious behavior

Sample: RANSOM_004 | Category: Ransomware
Evidence:
- [E1] (encryption) [E1] The malware encrypts files using RSA-2048 and AES-128 algorithms
- [E2] (persistence) [E2] It modifies the Windows Defender exclusion list to prevent detection
- [E3] (c2) [E3] The malware establishes communication with a remote server to download additional modules and receive instructions
- [E4] (privilege) [E4] It attempts to escalate privileges by exploiting known Windows vulnerabilities
- [E5] (filesystem) [E5] The ransomware targets a wide range of file extensions, including documents, images, and archives
